National-Masonry-VIC-Brochure-MDG-Book-1-Structural-Fire-Acoustic-v24 Canada: Seismic Design example: SEISMIC_DESIGN_GUIDE_FOR_MASONRY_BUILDIN Block Numbering System The New Zealand Concrete Masonry Association NZCMA has established the following national coding system The 20 Series refers to unit 200 mm nominal width To be able to satisfactorily design or construct a concrete masonry building or structure, it is necessary to understand the basics of masonry units: Modular dimensions These units are available in four different widths – nominally: 100 mm (10 series) 150 mm (15 series) 200 mm (20 series) – most commonly used unit 250 mm (25 series) Design of masonry veneers is addressed in some assistance may be provided by referring to AS 3700 ‘Masonry Structures’. Whilst all design philosophies are equally valid, general discussion amongst designers of concrete masonry Insulation: BRANZ Build106-28-InsulMasonryWalls Durability Exposure class as per NZS 3604 NZS 3604-2011+adv - Timber framed buildings , Page 64 Fire design: Chapter 5 of NZS 4230:2004 sets out the design requirements for fire resistance Concrete masonry is generally considered to have good fire resistance since it is non-combustible (i.e. it does not burn), absorbs heat only slowly, and does not give off toxic fumes or smoke. title: Waffle slab
text: Insulation Water tightness Monday, 3 February 2025 9:30 pm Waffle slab is designed as an alternative solution to AS 2870 as the clause 17.1.5 of NZS 3604 states that standard solutions of AS 2870 may be used for expansive soil. Reference documents: module-5-geotech-ground-improvement-version-1 , Page 85 canterbury-guidance-part-a AU: Paper158 Performance of standard waffle slab footing system under expansive soil movement T07_SENSE600-Design-Guide-for-Residential-Footings_Mar24 HB_28-1997 - The design of residential slabs and footings AS 2870-2011 - Residential slabs and footings C:\Users\ausfd0\OneDrive - WSP O365\6- Books\CCANZ Sample: APD-300-StormSlab-BC-Docs-Class-H1-AKL-up-to-three-Stories-1 1628487 NZ NZS 3604-2011+adv - Timber framed buildings Supplier Documents: Products: Firth - Masonry Homes Construction Manual [2009] Design process: HB_28-1997 - The design of residential slabs and footings 332007707-Residential-Footing-Design 407427950-27856-FC-A Insulation: Seismic Design module-5-geotech-ground-improvement-version-1 , Page 85 the building reinforced concrete wafﬂe slab is to be designed to withstand the following loss of support: 2m width from the edge, ie the raft is designed to cantilever 2 m at the edge. Any 4m wide area in the middle of slab, away from the edge. Load: ASNZS 1170.1-2002 Effective width of T beam NZS 3101, Page 117 Design example: ADA194571 Design to AS 2870: footing system design shall be by either prescribing a standard design in accordance with Section 3 ; or applying the engineering principles described in Section 4 ; and all design and construction shall comply with Sections 5 and 6 . title: CMB - Foundation
text: Thursday, 13 February 2025 4:01 pm NZS 4229-2013 Masonry Non-Specific Every masonry wall, whether loadbearing or non-loadbearing, shall be supported footing shall be formed symmetrically footing shall have all soil bearing surfaces horizontal the width of a footing shall not be less than 400 mm.

title: Wall Design
text: Wednesday, 19 February 2025 3:56 pm Design file: C:\Users\ausfd0\OneDrive - WSP O365\6- Books\Building\Masonry\NZ\SpreadsheetMasonary Reference documents: New Zealand Concrete Masonry Manual 15 vs 20 series Note however that the use of solid grout-filling or increased wall thickness has only minimal influence on bracing capacity, such that the provision of internal masonry walls or changes to the geometry of wall openings may instead be a more appropriate design modification. Partial vs solid Unless solid-filled masonry is required for other reasons, it is recommended that partial grout-filling be assumed at the beginning of the design.
